abstract: Detecting and localizing objects in space is a fundamental computer vision problem. While much progress has been made to solve 2D object detection, 3D object localization is much less explored and far from solved, especially for open-world categories. To address this research challenge, we propose Boxer, an algorithm to estimate static 3D bounding boxes (3DBBs) from 2D open-vocabulary object detections, posed images and optional depth either represented as a sparse point cloud or dense depth. At its core is BoxerNet, a transformer-based network which lifts 2D bounding box (2DBB) proposals into 3D, followed by multi-view fusion and geometric filtering to produce globally consistent de-duplicated 3DBBs in metric world space. Boxer leverages the power of existing 2DBB detection algorithms (e.g. DETIC, OWLv2, SAM3) to localize objects in 2D. This allows the main BoxerNet model to focus on lifting to 3D rather than detecting, ultimately reducing the demand for costly annotated 3DBB training data. Extending the CuTR formulation, we incorporate an aleatoric uncertainty for robust regression, a median depth patch encoding to support sparse depth inputs, and large-scale training with over 1.2 million unique 3DBBs. BoxerNet outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in open-world 3DBB lifting, including CuTR in egocentric settings without dense depth (0.532 vs. 0.010 mAP) and on CA-1M with dense depth available (0.412 vs. 0.250 mAP).

citations.fullCitation: 21. Lazarow, J., Griffiths, D., Kohavi, G., Crespo, F., Dehghan, A.: Cubify anything: Scaling indoor 3d object detection. arXiv preprint arXiv:2412.04458 (2024)
citations.justification: This work, which introduces the CuTR model and the CA-1M dataset, is the most critical citation. The Boxer paper explicitly states its model is an extension of the CuTR architecture and uses it as the primary baseline for performance comparison throughout the experiments.
citations.title: Cubify anything: Scaling indoor 3d object detection

citations.fullCitation: 6. Brazil, G., Kumar, A., Straub, J., Ravi, N., Johnson, J., Gkioxari, G.: Omni3D: A large benchmark and model for 3D object detection in the wild. In: Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR). pp. 9630–9641 (2023)
citations.justification: This paper introduces the Omni3D benchmark, a key dataset used to evaluate Boxer's performance. It also proposes Cube-RCNN, which is used as a baseline method for comparison in the experimental section, making this reference crucial for both evaluation and context.
citations.title: Omni3D: A large benchmark and model for 3D object detection in the wild

citations.fullCitation: 31. Minderer, M., Gritsenko, A., Houlsby, N.: Scaling open-vocabulary object detection. In: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems. vol. 36, pp. 72832–72859 (2023)
citations.justification: This citation is for OWLv2, an open-vocabulary 2D object detector. It is fundamentally important because Boxer's pipeline is built upon using such off-the-shelf 2D detectors as its first stage. The experiments extensively use OWLv2 to generate the initial 2D boxes that Boxer then lifts to 3D.
citations.title: Scaling open-vocabulary object detection

citations.fullCitation: 50. Yang, Y.H., Piccinelli, L., Segu, M., Li, S., Huang, R., Fu, Y., Pollefeys, M., Blum, H., Bauer, Z.: 3d-mood: Lifting 2d to 3d for monocular open-set object detection. In: Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision. pp. 7429–7439 (2025)
citations.justification: This paper presents a similar and competing approach for lifting 2D detections to 3D. It is used as a key state-of-the-art baseline in the experimental results (Table 2) to demonstrate the performance improvements offered by the Boxer model.
citations.title: 3d-mood: Lifting 2d to 3d for monocular open-set object detection

citations.fullCitation: 54. Zhou, X., Girdhar, R., Joulin, A., Krähenbühl, P., Misra, I.: Detecting twenty-thousand classes using image-level supervision. In: Proceedings of the European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV) (2022)
citations.justification: This paper introduces DETIC, one of the primary off-the-shelf 2D object detectors explicitly mentioned in the abstract and used in the Boxer pipeline. This citation is significant as it represents the foundational 2D detection stage that Boxer decouples from and builds upon to achieve open-world 3D lifting.
citations.title: Detecting twenty-thousand classes using image-level supervision

intermediateReport:
## Research Report: Boxer - Robust Lifting of Open-World 2D Bounding Boxes to 3D

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research paper "Boxer: Robust Lifting of Open-World 2D Bounding Boxes to 3D" was authored by Daniel DeTone, Tianwei Shen, Fan Zhang, Lingni Ma, Julian Straub, Richard Newcombe, and Jakob Engel. All authors are affiliated with Meta Reality Labs Research.

### 2. Broader Research Landscape

Detecting and localizing objects in three-dimensional (3D) space is a fundamental problem in computer vision with applications in areas such as contextual artificial intelligence (AI), robotic embodiment, augmented reality (AR), and digital twins. While significant advancements have been achieved in 2D object detection, particularly with "open-world" capabilities that allow recognition of a vast array of object categories, 3D object localization, especially for open-world scenarios, remains less explored and presents notable challenges.

A primary obstacle in 3D object detection is the disparity in data annotation compared to 2D. Generating metric 3D bounding box (3DBB) annotations requires specialized equipment like LiDAR, depth sensors, or calibrated multi-view systems, making data collection and labeling substantially more resource-intensive than for 2D bounding boxes. Consequently, 3D datasets are considerably smaller and less diverse than their 2D counterparts, limiting the ability of 3D models to generalize to the "long tail" of open-world objects. The existing 3D data landscape is also fragmented, incorporating various sensor modalities (dense depth maps, sparse point clouds) and camera models (pinhole, fisheye, panoramic).

Current approaches in 3D detection can be broadly categorized. "Closed-set" 3D detection methods, such as Cube-RCNN, H3DNet, VoteNet, 3DETR, EVL, and DETR3D, typically focus on a predefined, limited set of object categories and often rely heavily on dedicated 3D supervision. Some of these methods are sensitive to specific camera models or sensor modalities, requiring substantial adaptation for transfer across different settings. Conversely, "open-world 2D detection" has seen substantial progress, with models like DETIC, OWLv2, and SAM3 capable of detecting thousands of categories using text prompts, leveraging internet-scale image-text data.

"Open-world 3D detection" is an emerging field. Recent developments include generative approaches that treat localization as a "next-token prediction" task, leveraging vision-language models (VLMs) as seen in LocateAnything3D or N3D-VLM. However, these often face issues with 3D hallucinations and a lack of metric precision. Other approaches, like 3D-MOOD, DetAny3D, and OVMono3D, lift open-world 2D detections to 3D but are often limited to monocular images or specific depth processing mechanisms. CuTR [21] is identified as a particularly close baseline, training a DETR-style 3DBB detector using open-world 3DBB data, but it requires dense depth input and a specialized architecture. SAM3D focuses on full 3D shape and texture estimation rather than just bounding boxes.

The research identifies a gap: the absence of a universal interface for 3D perception that effectively combines powerful 2D semantics with metric 3D geometry, while remaining adaptable to diverse geometric cues (dense or sparse depth) and camera models. Boxer aims to address this by decoupling 2D localization from 3D lifting, leveraging mature open-world 2D detectors for the former and focusing model capacity on the latter.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary motivation for developing Boxer stems from the challenges in achieving robust, open-world 3D object localization, which is crucial for advancing contextual AI and embodiment applications. These applications, including autonomous navigation, dexterous manipulation, immersive AR, and digital twins, require a metric 3D understanding of objects (identity, scale, orientation, proximity) that is not adequately supported by current 3DBB estimation methods.

Specific challenges motivating this work include:
*   **Data Annotation Disparity:** The high cost and specialized setups required for 3DBB data annotation lead to significantly smaller and less diverse 3D datasets compared to 2D. This limits the "open-world" capability of 3D models.
*   **Closed-Set Limitations:** Many existing 3D detection methods are confined to a fixed, closed set of object categories due to reliance on closed-set 3D supervision.
*   **Fragmented Data Landscape:** Current 3D methods struggle with the heterogeneity of available 3D data, which includes varying sensor modalities (dense depth, sparse point clouds) and camera models (pinhole, fisheye). Architectures often bake in assumptions about these inputs, hindering generalization.
*   **Lack of Integration:** There is a perceived lack of a universal interface that effectively bridges the semantic power of 2D vision-language models with the geometric requirements of 3D perception, adaptable to diverse geometric cues and camera types.

Against this background, the key objectives of the Boxer research are:
*   **Develop an algorithm for estimating static, global 3D bounding boxes (3DBBs) for open-world object categories.** This involves taking posed images, off-the-shelf 2D bounding box (2DBB) open-world detections, and optional depth information (sparse point cloud or dense depth) as input.
*   **Decompose the 3D detection problem into two stages: 2D object localization and subsequent metric 3D lifting.** This aims to leverage the broad semantic coverage and strong localization capabilities of existing internet-scale 2D detectors, allowing the core 3D model to focus on geometric lifting rather than detection.
*   **Introduce BoxerNet, a transformer-based network designed to lift 2D bounding box proposals into 3D.** This network is intended to improve upon existing architectures like CuTR by incorporating features such as an aleatoric uncertainty head and a median depth patch encoding to support diverse depth inputs and enable large-scale training.
*   **Achieve multi-view fusion and geometric filtering to produce globally consistent and de-duplicated 3DBBs in metric world space.** This involves aggregating per-frame predictions into a stable scene-level representation.
*   **Reduce the demand for costly annotated 3DBB training data** by relying on readily available 2D detections and focusing 3D supervision on the lifting task.
*   **Ensure flexibility and robustness to various input conditions,** including different camera models (pinhole, fisheye) and geometric cues (with or without sparse point clouds or dense depth maps).

### 4. Methodology and Approach

Boxer is designed as a complete algorithm that processes a set of posed, calibrated images, potentially augmented with depth information, to generate a final set of global, metric 3DBBs for open-set objects. The pipeline consists of three main stages: 2D object detection, 2D-to-3D lifting via BoxerNet, and multi-view temporal fusion.

#### 4.1 2D Detection

The initial step in the Boxer pipeline involves utilizing an off-the-shelf open-world 2D bounding box (2DBB) detector. Text prompts are provided to these detectors (e.g., DETIC, OWLv2, SAM3) to localize specified objects within the input images. The output is a set of 2D bounding boxes, each parameterized by its corner coordinates and an associated 2D confidence score. The system is designed to handle grayscale images by channel repetition.

#### 4.2 BoxerNet: Lifting 2D to 3D

BoxerNet serves as the core module for lifting 2D detections to 3D. For each 2D detection, BoxerNet predicts a 7-Degrees of Freedom (DoF) 3DBB, parameterized by its center coordinates (x, y, z), physical extent (width, height, depth), and a single rotation (yaw) about the gravity axis. Additionally, each prediction is associated with a 3D confidence score. The 7-DoF representation is chosen due to common availability of gravity estimates from IMUs.

##### Lifting Encoder
The lifting model is implemented as a self-attention encoder that integrates visual appearance, scene depth, and camera calibration.
*   **Visual Features:** Dense visual features are extracted from the input image using a pretrained DINOv3 backbone.
*   **Depth Features:** To incorporate metric scale, optional depth input (sparse point clouds or dense depth) is projected into the image plane. The median depth within each image patch is computed to create a per-patch depth feature. Patches without projected points are assigned a value of -1. This median depth patch encoding is a departure from prior work like CuTR, which required a specialized ViT for dense depth.
*   **Ray Features:** Camera calibration and pose information are encoded as 3D ray features for each image patch, representing the normalized 3D ray from the camera associated with the patch center in the gravity-aligned coordinate frame. Camera translation is implicitly handled by setting the camera origin to (0,0,0).
These three modalities (visual, depth, ray) are concatenated per patch and fed as tokens into a shared self-attention encoder, allowing interaction and mixing of semantic, geometric, and calibration information.

##### 2D-to-3D Box Decoder
Following the self-attention encoding, 3D predictions are generated by conditioning on the 2D detections via cross-attention. Each 2D box is linearly projected to a feature dimension consistent with the patch tokens, and then cross-attended over all encoder tokens (patches) independently. This design ensures permutation invariance regarding the order of 2D box inputs. The resulting box-specific latent representation is then passed to two prediction heads:
*   **7-DoF Bounding Box Head:** Regresses the 3D bounding box parameters.
*   **Aleatoric Uncertainty Head:** Predicts an aleatoric uncertainty value, capturing observational noise and ambiguity in the lifting process. This is an improvement over CuTR, which couples 2D and 3D detection scores.

The final confidence score for each box is calculated as the mean of its 2D detection confidence and its 3D prediction confidence, which is derived from the predicted aleatoric uncertainty. This combined score is used for ranking detections during inference.

##### Training Objective
BoxerNet is trained using a loss function that combines geometric alignment with aleatoric uncertainty modeling. The primary geometric loss is a Chamfer loss computed between the predicted and ground-truth 3D box corners. This is modulated by an exponential of the negative predicted aleatoric uncertainty, with the uncertainty term also added directly to the loss.

#### 4.3 Multi-View Temporal Fusion

The final stage aims to produce a set of de-duplicated, global, static 3DBBs for the entire scene by aggregating per-frame estimates across time and views. This is achieved through a multi-stage geometric and semantic fusion pipeline:
*   **3D IoU Filtering:** Per-frame 3D detections are initially filtered based on a 3D Intersection-over-Union (IoU) threshold (default 0.3) to restrict fusion to geometrically overlapping boxes.
*   **Semantic Filtering:** Semantic consistency is enforced by requiring similar category labels (computed via text embeddings) for detections to be merged, preventing fusion of spatially overlapping but semantically distinct objects.
*   **Clustering:** An undirected graph is constructed where nodes are detections and edges connect geometrically and semantically compatible pairs. Connected components analysis identifies clusters, each hypothesized to correspond to a single physical object observed across multiple frames.
*   **Rotation-Aware Averaging:** Within each cluster, confidence-weighted fusion is performed. To address the 90° rotational symmetry of gravity-aligned boxes, detections are first aligned to a canonical orientation before averaging position, dimensions, and yaw (using a circular mean).
*   **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS):** Finally, 3D NMS (default IoU threshold 0.6) is applied to the fused hypotheses to remove any remaining duplicate detections, yielding a compact set of scene-level 3D object detections. The paper also discusses an O(N) online tracking mode for longer sequences, though offline processing is primarily used for experiments.

#### 4.4 Training Dataset

Boxer is trained on a large and diverse dataset comprising both internal and public sources, accumulating approximately 1.22 million unique 3DBBs across 42.1 million image views.
*   **Internal Data:** Project Aria (Gen1 and Gen2) and Quest3 data.
*   **Public Data:** NymeriaPlus, CA-1M, and portions of Omni3D (specifically SUN-RGBD and ScanNet 3DBBs from Scan2CAD). ARKit Scenes, Objectron, and Hypersim are excluded due to overlap or specific dataset characteristics.
The emphasis is on "unique 3D bounding boxes" to accurately represent dataset scale, distinguishing from mere repeated observations in posed video.

#### 4.5 Per-View Visibility and Data Augmentation

Since not all 3DBBs are visible in every frame of a posed video, per-frame visibility is computed. This involves checking if at least two visible depth points lie inside the 3DBB and if 80% of sampled points along the box edges are visible within the valid image region, accounting for occlusion.

To enhance robustness and generalization, four types of data augmentation are applied to the encoder inputs:
*   **Photometric Augmentation:** Standard variations in brightness, contrast, sharpness, Gaussian blur, and gamma.
*   **Camera Augmentation:** Random perturbations of camera center and focal length to encourage invariance to calibration noise and support fisheye/non-fisheye distortions.
*   **Depth Augmentation:** Randomly dropping all points, individual points, or contiguous blocks of points from the projected 3D points/dense depth to simulate sparsity and partial observability.
*   **2D Box Augmentation:** To address loose 2D bounding boxes derived from 3D projections, a segmentation model (SAM) is used to derive tighter 2D bounding boxes from projected 2D masks, with added Gaussian noise for robustness.

#### 4.6 Training and Inference Details

BoxerNet is trained for approximately two weeks on 16 H100 GPUs using the AdamW optimizer with a cosine-decaying learning rate. A forward pass for 2DBB lifting on 960x960 images takes approximately 20 ms on an NVIDIA RTX 4090. The model has about 25 million trainable parameters (excluding DINOv3 backbone).

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The experiments evaluate Boxer’s performance in per-frame single-image 3D detection, per-scene fused 3D detection, and through sensitivity studies. Evaluation metrics primarily include class-agnostic mean Average Precision (mAP) computed over a range of 3D IoU thresholds ([0.05, 0.5]).

#### 5.1 Per-Frame Experimental Analysis

(See Table 2 in the original paper for detailed metrics)

*   **BoxerNet consistently improves 3DBB estimation** across various off-the-shelf 2D bounding box detectors (DETIC, 3D-MOOD, CubeRCNN, OWLv2). When used with a given 2DBB input, BoxerNet generally outperforms other lifting mechanisms or direct 3DBB detectors.
*   **Significant Outperformance over CuTR:** BoxerNet demonstrates notable improvements compared to the CuTR baseline. For instance, on the NymeriaPlus dataset (egocentric, sparse point cloud depth), BoxerNet achieves 0.296 mAP (RGB with GT2D) versus CuTR's 0.010 mAP. On the CA-1M dataset (dense depth available), BoxerNet achieves 0.412 mAP (RGB+D with GT2D) compared to CuTR's 0.250 mAP. These results hold across different 2DBB detector inputs.
*   **Comparison with 3D-MOOD:** BoxerNet also shows improved performance over 3D-MOOD in both image-only and image+depth settings. This is attributed, in part, to BoxerNet’s DINOv3 backbone, which provides robust monocular depth priors, as opposed to 3D-MOOD’s approach of learning an auxiliary depth map on a smaller dataset.
*   **Qualitative Results:** Visualizations (Figure 4) support the quantitative findings, showing that Boxer’s predictions have higher 3D IoU overlap with ground truth boxes, indicated by more yellow-colored predictions.

#### 5.2 Per-Scene Experimental Analysis

(See Table 3 in the original paper for detailed metrics)

*   **Consistent Improvement in Fused Results:** The trends observed in per-frame evaluation extend to per-scene (fused) 3D detection. BoxerNet consistently improves performance over CuTR across video datasets such as NymeriaPlus, ADT, and CA-1M, in both RGB-only and RGB+Depth configurations.
*   For example, on NymeriaPlus (RGB), OWLv2+BoxerNet achieves 0.145 mAP compared to OWLv2+CuTR's 0.013 mAP. With RGB+Depth inputs, OWLv2+BoxerNet achieves 0.278 mAP on NymeriaPlus, 0.215 mAP on ADT, and 0.204 mAP on CA-1M, generally outperforming CuTR equivalents where available.
*   **Temporal Consistency:** Qualitative visualizations (Figure 5) illustrate that Boxer produces more consistent predictions across frames, leading to a "sharper heatmap" when per-frame 3DBBs are aggregated into a global map, indicating greater stability than CuTR.

#### 5.3 Ablation and Sensitivity Studies

(See Table 4 in the original paper for detailed metrics)

*   **Impact of Depth Input:** The availability of depth information (sparse point clouds) significantly influences performance. Removing depth input from BoxerNet led to a substantial drop in mAP on NymeriaPlus (from 0.518 to 0.279).
*   **Aleatoric Uncertainty:** The aleatoric uncertainty head contributes to model robustness, as its removal resulted in a performance decrease (0.518 to 0.485 mAP on NymeriaPlus).
*   **Training Data Scale and Diversity:** Training only on a single public dataset (e.g., CA-1M) shows a large domain gap when tested on other datasets (e.g., NymeriaPlus mAP drops from 0.518 to 0.002). The inclusion of internal data is beneficial, as removing it reduced performance on both NymeriaPlus (0.518 to 0.463) and CA-1M (0.412 to 0.376).
*   **Confidence Score Blending:** Experiments on CA-1M show that averaging the 2D and 3D confidence scores provides the strongest performance for ranking detections, outperforming using either score in isolation (Figure 6).
*   **Comparison to SAM3+SAM3D:** On a subset of 100 ADT images with a reduced taxonomy, SAM3+BoxerNet (RGB+Depth) achieved 0.064 mAP, outperforming SAM3+SAM3D (0.027 mAP), indicating more accurate layout estimation for 3DBBs (Table 5).
*   **mAP by Object Size:** Analysis of precision-recall curves by object volume on CA-1M (Figure 10) reveals that BoxerNet’s performance advantage over CuTR is most prominent for small objects, which are also the most frequently occurring.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

Boxer represents an advancement in the field of open-world 3D object detection, addressing key limitations of existing methods. Its significance and potential impact can be summarized as follows:

*   **Bridging 2D and 3D Perception:** Boxer effectively combines the semantic richness and broad coverage of advanced open-world 2D object detectors with the geometric precision required for 3D localization. This decomposition allows for leveraging the scalability of 2D supervision while focusing model capacity on the challenging 3D lifting task.
*   **Reduced Data Annotation Dependence:** By building upon existing 2D detection capabilities, Boxer reduces the intensive requirement for costly, specialized 3D bounding box annotations, which historically has been a bottleneck for open-world 3D perception.
*   **Robustness to Diverse Inputs:** The BoxerNet architecture, particularly with its median depth patch encoding, demonstrates robustness to various geometric input modalities (dense depth maps or sparse point clouds) and camera types (pinhole or fisheye). This adaptability broadens its applicability across different sensing platforms and scenarios.
*   **Enhanced 3D Bounding Box Accuracy:** The quantitative and qualitative results consistently indicate that BoxerNet outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, including CuTR, in both per-frame and per-scene 3DBB lifting, particularly in egocentric settings and for smaller objects. The multi-view temporal fusion further enhances consistency and reduces duplication.
*   **Enabling Advanced AI Applications:** The ability to reliably estimate static, global, and de-duplicated 3DBBs for open-world objects is fundamental for several emerging AI domains. This includes enabling contextual AI agents to reason about objects in their metric 3D environment, supporting goal-directed autonomous navigation and dexterous manipulation for robots, and facilitating seamless human-machine collaboration in immersive augmented reality and digital twin applications.
*   **Practical and Scalable Solution:** Boxer provides a practical and scalable approach for constructing consistent scene-level 3D object representations from posed image data. The demonstrated efficiency of BoxerNet and the release of code and models facilitate its adoption and further research.
*   **Foundation for Future Research:** BoxerNet can be utilized as a direct replacement for 3DBB estimation modules in existing 2D or 3D object detection pipelines. Its capability to generate dense pseudo-annotations on closed-world datasets (e.g., ScanNet) also offers a pathway to enrich existing datasets and potentially support the training of larger vision-language models with improved 3D understanding.
overview:
Detecting and localizing objects in three-dimensional (3D) space is a cornerstone of modern computer vision, powering applications from autonomous robots to augmented reality (AR) headsets. While 2D object detection has reached high levels of maturity—allowing models to recognize thousands of categories from "open-world" prompts—3D object localization remains a difficult frontier. The primary bottleneck is data: while 2D images are abundant on the internet, metric 3D data requires specialized sensors like LiDAR or depth cameras and complex calibration, making it expensive to collect and label.

**Boxer** is a research framework designed to bridge this gap. It introduces a robust method for "lifting" 2D bounding boxes into 3D world space. By decoupling the semantic recognition of an object (the 2D part) from its geometric localization (the 3D part), Boxer leverages powerful off-the-shelf 2D detectors to handle the "open-world" variety of objects while using a specialized network, **BoxerNet**, to estimate their 3D physical size and position.

![Boxer Algorithm Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05212v1/boxernetv12.jpg)
*Figure 1: The Boxer pipeline takes posed images and text prompts as input, uses a 2D detector to find objects, and then "lifts" those detections into metric 3D space using BoxerNet before fusing them across multiple views.*

## The Architecture of 3D Lifting

The Boxer algorithm operates in three distinct stages: open-world 2D detection, 2D-to-3D lifting via BoxerNet, and multi-view temporal fusion. This modular approach is designed to be "universal," meaning it can work with different camera types (like fisheye or pinhole) and various types of depth cues (dense depth maps or sparse point clouds).

### 1. Open-World 2D Detection
The process begins with a standard 2D detector such as OWLv2 or DETIC. A user provides text prompts (e.g., "chair," "guitar," "coffee table"), and the detector outputs 2D bounding boxes in the image plane along with a confidence score $c_{2d} \in [0, 1]$. Because these 2D models are trained on massive internet-scale datasets, they can find objects that a traditional 3D-only model might have never seen.

### 2. BoxerNet: The Lifting Engine
BoxerNet is the heart of the system. It is a transformer-based network that takes a 2D box and the surrounding image context and predicts a 7-Degrees of Freedom (7-DoF) 3D bounding box. This 3D box is defined by its center $(x, y, z)$, its physical extent $(w, h, d)$, and its rotation $\theta$ (yaw) around the gravity axis. 

Unlike previous models that required specific types of depth input, BoxerNet uses a flexible **Median Depth Patch Encoding**. The image is divided into patches, and for each patch, the model calculates the median depth value from whatever geometric data is available (like a sparse point cloud). This allows the model to "fill in the gaps" when depth data is noisy or sparse.

![BoxerNet Architecture](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05212v1/boxerv12.jpg)
*Figure 2: BoxerNet integrates visual features from a DINOv3 backbone with ray features and depth information. It uses cross-attention to condition the 3D output on the initial 2D bounding box proposals.*

### 3. Multi-View Temporal Fusion
A single 2D detection from one frame might be slightly inaccurate. To solve this, Boxer aggregates predictions across time and different camera views. It clusters detections that represent the same physical object and averages their position, size, and orientation. This step ensures that if a user walks around a room with an AR headset, the "3D chair" they see remains stable in the same spot, rather than flickering or drifting.

## Handling Ambiguity with Aleatoric Uncertainty

Lifting a 2D box to 3D is inherently ambiguous. For example, a small object close to the camera can look exactly like a large object far away. To handle this, BoxerNet predicts **aleatoric uncertainty**—a measure of how "unsure" the model is about its geometric prediction due to observational noise.

The model outputs a 3D confidence score $c_{3d} \in [0, 1] \text{ where } c_{3d} = \exp(-\sigma_{box})$ and $\sigma_{box} \in \mathbb{R}$ is the predicted uncertainty. This is combined with the 2D detector's confidence to create a final, robust ranking score:

$$\bar{c} = \frac{c_{2d} + c_{3d}}{2}$$

This score is crucial during inference. If the 2D detector is sure it found a "mug" but BoxerNet is unsure about its 3D location (perhaps due to motion blur or low light), the final confidence will be lower, preventing the system from placing "hallucinated" objects in the 3D map.

The training loss function $L$ also incorporates this uncertainty, allowing the model to "down-weight" examples it finds geometrically confusing:

$$L = L_{box} \exp(-\sigma_{box}) + \sigma_{box}$$

Where $L_{box}$ is the geometric error (Chamfer loss) between the predicted and ground-truth 3D box corners.

## Innovation in Feature Encoding

A key technical contribution of Boxer is how it represents the camera's geometry to the transformer. It uses **Ray Features**, which encode the 3D direction of the light rays hitting every patch of the image. This is calculated using the camera's calibration and its pose relative to gravity. By feeding these rays directly into the transformer as tokens, the model learns to understand the perspective of different camera lenses—including distorted fisheye lenses—without needing specialized mathematical adjustments for each camera type.

Combined with a pretrained **DINOv3** backbone for visual features, BoxerNet achieves a deep understanding of both "what" an object is and "where" it should sit in a 3D scene.

## Experimental Performance

The researchers tested Boxer on several diverse datasets, including egocentric video from Project Aria and public datasets like CA-1M and ADT. A frequent baseline for comparison was **CuTR**, a prior DETR-style 3D detector. 

Results showed that BoxerNet consistently outperformed existing methods. For instance, on the NymeriaPlus dataset—which features egocentric video with very sparse point clouds—Boxer achieved a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 0.296, while the CuTR baseline only reached 0.010. This massive performance gap highlights Boxer's superior ability to handle real-world, noisy data.

![3D Detection Results on SUN-RGBD](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05212v1/sunrgbd_boxermood2drgb.jpg)
*Figure 3: Qualitative results showing Boxer's ability to accurately lift 2D detections into 3D boxes that align well with the actual room geometry (shown here on the SUN-RGBD dataset).*

Boxer also proved particularly effective at localizing **small objects**, which are notoriously difficult for 3D sensors. Because it relies on high-resolution 2D detections to start the process, it can "pinpoint" small items like light switches or remote controls that traditional 3D-only detectors might miss.

## Impact and Applications

The significance of Boxer lies in its **universal interface** for 3D perception. By allowing any 2D detector to be transformed into a 3D detector, it democratizes 3D perception for developers who don't have access to massive 3D datasets.

Some of the potential applications include:
*   **Contextual AI:** Helping AI assistants understand exactly where objects are in a room (e.g., "The keys are on the table to your left").
*   **Robotic Embodiment:** Providing robots with the metric coordinates needed to grasp or avoid objects in unknown environments.
*   **Digital Twins:** Rapidly creating 3D maps of real-world spaces by simply walking through them with a camera.

By effectively combining 2D semantics with 3D metric geometry, Boxer provides a scalable and robust path toward machines that can "see" and "reason" about the 3D world as naturally as humans do.

keyInsights: Decoupling 2D localization from 3D lifting allows for leveraging the scalability and broad semantic coverage of existing open-world 2D detectors, while focusing the core model's capacity on precise 3D geometric estimation.

The median depth patch encoding strategy, combined with an aleatoric uncertainty head within BoxerNet's transformer architecture, enables robust performance across various depth input types (sparse or dense) and camera models.

Effective multi-view temporal fusion, incorporating both geometric and semantic consistency, is crucial for synthesizing stable, de-duplicated 3D object representations from individual frame predictions into a coherent scene-level understanding.
originalProblem: Open-world 3D object localization is hindered by the high cost and limited availability of metric 3D bounding box annotations compared to readily available 2D data.
Existing 'closed-set' 3D detection methods are often restricted to predefined categories, sensitive to specific camera models or sensor modalities, and struggle with diverse 3D data.
There is a lack of a universal interface that effectively integrates powerful open-world 2D semantics with precise metric 3D geometry, adaptable to various geometric cues and camera types.
results: BoxerNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, including CuTR and 3D-MOOD, in per-frame 3D bounding box estimation, achieving significant mAP improvements (e.g., 0.296 mAP vs. CuTR's 0.010 mAP on NymeriaPlus with RGB and ground truth 2D boxes).

The multi-view temporal fusion stage further enhances performance in per-scene 3D detection, producing more stable and consistent global 3DBBs across datasets (e.g., 0.278 mAP on NymeriaPlus with RGB+Depth for BoxerNet with OWLv2, compared to 0.013 mAP for CuTR).

Ablation studies demonstrate the importance of depth input, the aleatoric uncertainty head, and large-scale, diverse training data for Boxer's robustness, with its performance advantage over baselines being most pronounced for smaller, more frequently occurring objects.
solution: The 3D detection problem is decomposed into two stages: utilizing off-the-shelf open-world 2D detectors for initial localization, followed by a dedicated metric 3D lifting process.

A transformer-based network, BoxerNet, is introduced to lift 2D bounding box proposals into 7-DoF 3D bounding boxes, integrating visual features, median depth patch encoding (for diverse depth inputs), and camera calibration information.

A multi-view temporal fusion pipeline aggregates per-frame 3D predictions into globally consistent, de-duplicated 3DBBs for the entire scene, employing geometric and semantic filtering, clustering, and rotation-aware averaging.
summary.summary: Boxer introduces an algorithm that robustly lifts open-world 2D bounding box detections to static, global 3D bounding boxes in metric world space. Its transformer-based BoxerNet, trained on a large dataset, improves 3D bounding box estimation over prior methods, particularly for smaller objects, across diverse inputs like sparse point clouds or dense depth maps.

title: Boxer: Robust Lifting of Open-World 2D Bounding Boxes to 3D
